sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Sepsis is life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. Septic shock is the subset of sepsis with profound circulatory and cellular or metabolic abnormalities, usually presenting with persistent hypoperfusion, hypotension, or both, and carries substantially increased mortality.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Red Flag Sepsis** - high deterioration risk, organ dysfunction present

**Adults (≥12 years) - Red Flag if ANY present:**
- Objective evidence of new/altered mental status
- SBP ≤90 mmHg or >40 mmHg drop from baseline
- Heart rate ≥130 bpm
- Temperature ≤35°C
- Respiratory rate ≥25 breaths/min
- Oxygen required to maintain SpO₂ ≥94% (≥88% in COPD)
- Non-blanching rash, mottling, pallor, or cyanosis
- No urine output for 18+ hours (<0.5 mL/kg/hr if catheterised)
- Lactate ≥2 mmol/L
- Recent chemotherapy

**Children <5 years - Red Flag if ANY present:**
- BGL <3.0 mmol/L (neonates <2.6 mmol/L)
- Unresponsiveness or difficulty rousing
- Appears unwell to healthcare provider
- Respiratory rate: ≥75/min (<12 months), ≥55/min (1–5 years)
- Heart rate: ≥180/min (<12 months), ≥170/min (1–5 years)
- SBP: ≤50 mmHg (<3 months), ≤60 mmHg (3 months–5 years)
- SpO₂ <88% or O₂ requirement ≥10 L/min
- Non-blanching rash
- Lactate >4 mmol/L

**Children 5–11 years - Red Flag if ANY present:**
- BGL ≤3.0 mmol/L
- Unresponsiveness or difficulty rousing
- Objective altered mental status
- Respiratory rate ≥50/min; Heart rate ≥170 bpm or bradycardia <60 bpm
- SBP <60 mmHg
- SpO₂ <88% or O₂ requirement >10 L/min
- Non-blanching rash; Lactate >4 mmol/L

**Pregnant patients:** Same adult Red Flag criteria; note lactate may be elevated post-delivery

---

**Amber Flag Sepsis (Adults)** - moderate-to-high deterioration risk:
- Relative concern about mental status or acute functional decline
- Recent trauma, surgery, or procedure within 8 weeks
- Immunosuppression (cancer treatment, long-term steroids, diabetes, splenectomy)
- Respiratory rate 21–24/min
- Heart rate 91–129 bpm or new dysrhythmia
- SBP 91–100 mmHg
- Temperature 35.1–36°C or ≥38°C
- Clinical wound infection signs

**High-Risk Populations:**
- Age <1 year or >75 years; frail individuals
- Impaired immunity (cancer, diabetes, sickle cell, chronic steroids, immunosuppressants)
- Recent surgery or invasive procedure within 6 weeks
- Skin breach (cuts, burns, blisters, infected wounds)
- IV drug use; indwelling lines or catheters

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Identify source of infection: Pneumonia, urinary tract infection, intra-abdominal infection, skin/soft tissue infection, indwelling devices, or toxin-mediated sepsis

**People with impaired immune systems:**
  - people being treated for cancer with chemotherapy
  - people with diabetes, people who have had a splenectomy, or people with sickle cell disease, ongoing illnesses or misuse drugs intravenously
  - people taking long-term steroids
  - people taking immunosuppressant drugs to treat non-malignant disorders such as rheumatoid arthritis
  - people who have had surgery, or other invasive procedures, in the past 6 weeks
  - people with any breach of skin integrity (for example, cuts, burns, blisters or skin infections)
  - Pregnant women who have given birth, had a termination of pregnancy or miscarriage, or have continued vaginal bleeding or offensive discharge (consider prolonged rupture of membranes, retained products of conception, caesarean section, etc.)
  - People who have or have been in close contact with people with group A streptococcal infection, for example, scarlet fever
  - Monitor for rapid deterioration: Septic shock can progress quickly; frequent reassessment is critical

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Oxygen only as clinically indicated
- Perform a focused A to E assessment and frequent reassessment
- Use sepsis screening tools as an adjunct only
- Do not rely on qSOFA alone to exclude or confirm sepsis
- Maintain a high index of suspicion in any patient with suspected infection plus abnormal vital signs, altered mental state, poor perfusion, or clinical deterioration
- Minimise scene time and arrange early hospital pre-notification when sepsis or septic shock is suspected
- Be prepared for rapid deterioration and possible cardiac arrest

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain IV access early, ideally two large-bore cannulas where feasible
- Apply cardiac monitoring
- Measure lactate where available if this does not delay care
- Consider crystalloid fluid resuscitation for hypotension or suspected hypoperfusion, with frequent reassessment for response and fluid overload
- Consider early IV antibiotics only in consultation with the on-call doctor and local protocol
- Escalate early for likely sepsis or septic shock
- Consider vasopressor support only in consultation with the on-call doctor and within local scope or infusion capability

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Screening tools and lactate are risk-stratification aids and should support, not replace, clinical judgement
- Normal blood pressure does not exclude evolving septic shock
- Sepsis management priorities in the prehospital setting are early recognition, supportive care, repeated reassessment, rapid transport, and early escalation
- If meningococcal disease is suspected, follow the dedicated meningococcal or meningitis guidance and local antimicrobial protocol
